data.title: Accenture: AI Story Isn't Enough To Excite Investors Just Yet
data.description:
Accenture (NYSE:ACN) is one of the top strategy, consulting and operations solutions globally. Accenture does have a solid moat and deep relationships with global top companies, but Accenture has been continuing to sell off due to growth fears with FY26 guidance only guiding for 2%-5% growth, which suggests that revenue is continuing to stagnate. AI acts as a dual edged sword for Accenture, though it could be more beneficial to Accenture since the adoption of AI would likely benefit Accenture with more demand. AI could also displace some of the repetitive parts of Accenture's business, though recent bullish sentiment has risen from this with the partnership with Mistral AI. Nevertheless, the current sentiment seems extremely negative with a suggestion of a slowdown in spending for Accenture's key segments, clearly reflected in their projected FY26 revenue growth. For investors who may be bullish on Accenture's future, this is also one of the best times to buy, though just rebounded from the oversold level after the Mistral AI news, Accenture's valuation still seems extremely cheap. Hence, there are many key things to look out for in the next quarter, as Accenture's future in this AI boom remains slightly unclear, as I rate Accenture at a 'Hold'.

Accenture's Moat

The most core part of Accenture's moat has always been the switching cost for firms, especially for managed services, as Accenture gets deeply integrated into operations, whereas switching over would be extremely costly and disrupt operations. Due to extremely high switching costs for businesses, the revenue Accenture receives are a reliable source of revenue that analysts can count on, as Accenture is an expert in key industries globally like financial services and health services. With companies continuing to outsource work especially more complex tech related segments such as cybersecurity and cloud, though always been a long-term stable revenue segment, it is currently thriving due to having a wide moat. With an increase in AI adoption over the coming years, existing relationships could hence also translate into consulting revenue, as once the consulting period starts for a specific project like AI integration, the switching cost for consulting may be high. However, consulting switching cost isn't extremely high, as there isn't a lock in period like managed services do, unless certain individuals/businesses simply trust and rely on Accenture way more individually.

Due to years and years of success, reliability and the deep long relationships with many corporations, Accenture continues to be a trusted name especially for decisions that risk major corporate transformation, as reputation in this type of business is extremely important. This is also one of the reasons switching costs are high, since Accenture may be the only provider for the complex services they need while they are looking for someone to trust. With Accenture also having deep relationships with the systems themselves seen above, this is also extremely important for enterprises that want to adopt new tech as Accenture essentially is able to implement it for them.

Another major factor of Accenture's moat is its scale, as having billions of dollars in revenue for major global segments like Americas, EMEA and APAC is hard to achieve, as well as still growing like in Q1 FY26. Though due to being labour heavy margins will by default take a hit compared to software heavy stocks, Accenture's scale and efficiency are hard to match for smaller local competitors as the reputation for these services plays a massive part. Though not impenetrable from this aspect, Accenture's services aren't all about just efficiency and margins, but also the trust and quality of service Accenture provides to get it done, as without that there is no point in Accenture's margins being high as there wouldn't even be sales anyways.

Q1 FY26 And What I'm Looking out for in Q2

Accenture's sentiment has been clear over the last year, with the market clearly disliking the current guidance for FY26 due to a poor revenue growth rate and a major weakness in consulting demand. Accenture isn't exactly the pure AI play as well, though AI exposure increasing (talk about this more later), talks of AI displacement similar to the current SaaS Apocalypse has inevitably also hit Accenture over the last year though Accenture clearly doesn't directly fit in that category, but the sentiment and thesis remains the same, AI is a problem for Accenture's business, at least some of it, such as potentially lower billable hours for certain work of the automation of managed services. As I talked about in my Salesforce article back in November, this is extremely similar and I believe very much a narrative issue, and that the narrative is further reinforced with poor expected revenue growth in FY26 following Q1 results.

Accenture's business continues to do well with 6% revenue growth in Q1, whilst revenues across all geographic markets continue to perform well. The only industry that is not growing is health and public service, which has been a huge concern to investors due to reduced government spending during the shutdown and cost cutting measures. This wasn't exactly unexpected, as the news came out around 1 year ago, but still a key thing to look out for in the next quarter regarding a potential bounce back as every other industry is still growing. I believe next quarter a key thing to look out for is health and public services revenue growth, as every other segment is doing fine. The slowdown could be temporary and hit Accenture in the short term, but the timeline for when the government will go back to Accenture is unclear, so a sudden bounce back could excite investors as revenue growth is evidently getting hit from the loss of government revenue.

A key swing potential is their consulting revenue, as managed services revenue is way more predictable and is currently doing extremely well, so the main focus goes back to consulting revenue. The main problem is with the poor guidance and good Q1 results, there are essentially 2 outcomes which are either management raises FY26 outlook or revenue actually slows down. With managed services being more reliable, I expect consulting revenue to be the one to get hit, so that is a key thing to look out for next quarter. Compared to IBM's consulting segment, which generates just over double Accenture's revenue, IBM grew 1% in the most recent quarter with a full year book to bill of 1.03, whilst Accenture had a book to bill of 1, so it does seem that consulting revenue will be rather flat over the next year. If consulting sees a rebound in 2026, which currently seems unlikely, it could be a major change in sentiment for Accenture. Managed services book to bill is currently at 1.2, so my main focus and worry is now on consulting, which I think is the key factor for Accenture as the revenue is way more cyclical and especially at this point in time.

As seen above, US federal business took a 1% hit in revenue growth, now expected to grow between 2% to 5%, which isn't the most optimistic when Q1 grew 6% YoY. This means that with $69.68B revenue in FY25, at a 3.5% growth rate the midpoint would suggest that FY26 revenue is $72.12B. With $18.74B in Q1, this means that the remaining 3 quarters Q2-Q4 FY25 to FY26 would only have 2.67% of growth, which obviously doesn't help the bull case from this perspective. With an adjusted operating margin of 15.6% in FY25, this means that FY26 will be another rather stagnant year, and that the sentiment will likely continue to be bearish unless management raises guidance, which I think is possible with Q1 growing already higher than the guided revenue growth.

AI is essentially Accenture's biggest catalyst over the next decade, and Accenture isn't exactly slowing down on expanding its AI momentum. As seen above, their advanced AI bookings grew 76% YoY with revenues more than double in Q1, as the projected TAM in 3 years is $70B.

I believe the key thing to look out for in their commentary next quarter is the replies to their recent acquisitions and partnerships like Mistral AI and Ookla. I have no doubt that analysts will be questioning Accenture on the AI side of the business, and that the numbers put up will be exciting and bullish, but the main question now is how much of that can actually translate back to their core segments, specifically more on the consulting side.

Accenture obviously has correctly identified that the AI momentum is very real and leaning into it, as AI does act as a dual edged sword and has a good chance of removing parts of Accenture's business, but the main question now is how Accenture is leaning into this and adapting for future growth. The relationship with Mistral AI to develop models and enterprise AI solutions, as this aims to adopt Mistral AI's models to lead transformation projects whether consulting and outsourcing. This clearly excited investors as the stock ticked up following the announcement. As they mention in the Ookla acquisition, they will now offer 'end-to-end network intelligence services essential for AI-based transformation'. The next phase of Accenture is clear, but the questions and focus are now on Accenture themselves to actually execute and create the product, as though they are already monetizing their AI agents, Accenture is clearly trying to create something even better than a reusable agent, but a model that can be reusable for corporations to implement AI easily, as well as improve metrics which could be a major game changer. Analysts will likely ask for more clarification in the next few quarters, so that is a key thing to look out for and the next catalyst/bull case for Accenture.

On top of the already mentioned narrative issue, poor consulting revenue/book to bill, as well as public sector spending slowdowns, their restructuring fee or what they call 'business optimization cost', with Q1 being $308M and the last 6 months totaling up to $923M. It was expected to come out at $250M, but the number actually came out at $58M more which is over 20% more than expected. When it comes to cutting costs and companies reducing their workforce, it doesn't take millions to reduce costs and should actually benefit them, which is not the case for Accenture here, as the primary cost to all was listed as employee severance. Though adjusted operating margins are not contracting, as is flat YoY, this should still be a major concern for investors as the extremely high cost here is simply not optimal long term.

Valuation

Accenture remains extremely attractive at its current valuations with a non-GAAP P/E of around 15, whilst P/S is around 1.8, which seems extremely cheap when compared to the 5Y averages. If their AI progress actually starts growing, their valuation from its current levels is no doubt extremely cheap, and Accenture is currently undervalued from this perspective.

However, I continue to believe that sentiment plays a major part of the whole thesis, like the whole SaaS selloff, good earnings doesn't directly lead to a higher stock price, as management's view of the future with their implementation of AI has to be positive and bullish, as until this narrative for investors changes, it is likely that the stock will continue to be depressed at its current levels.

Its current RSI also seems attractive, as Accenture's prolonged selloff led it deep in the RSI at a level of 20 (30 is oversold), which rebounded after the announcement of their partnership with Mistral AI which clearly excited investors. Being deep in the oversold level recently was evidently short lived with the announcement of the partnership with Mistral AI, as the current RSI level just sits directly above the oversold level. Though not in the oversold level, the stock is still extremely depressed and still seems slightly attractive at its current levels, as Accenture's RSI is just about the oversold zone. For investors perhaps wanting to start a position and finding for a good opportunity for an entry price, I believe that now shouldn't be considered a bad time.

Final Thoughts

Accenture's FY26 is set up to be a slightly weak year, which is shocking considering the increased implementation of AI into enterprises and that Q1 is an extremely strong quarter. I'm still slightly neutral on SaaS stocks as I believe the narrative part has to be clarified, though already seemingly extremely bullish, Accenture role in the future of AI have to be further clarified until I believe the stock will stop being depressed and return to its highs, but with ongoing AI fears as well as a weak FY26, though with seemingly bullish acquisitions and AI advancement, as well as some part of the stagnate growth being short term, I believe that Accenture requires further clarification for a rerating, though it does seem extremely cheap right now.

data.description:
Market Review

  • U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter and the full year higher, extending a multi-year rally. Positive factors for the markets included stronger-than-expected corporate earnings, continued consumer spending, and large investments in artificial intelligence ('AI') by major technology companies, alongside interest-rate cuts from the Federal Reserve. Easing trade tensions and ongoing merger activity also helped reinforce confidence in the economic outlook. Despite the strong quarter, investors grew more cautious about whether heavy AI spending will translate into profits, while signs of a softening job market and affordability pressures weighed on sentiment. Housing-related industries and several consumer-focused sectors lagged, highlighting growing concerns that economic strength is becoming less evenly shared. 1

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned 1.71%, reflecting performance at the net asset value ('NAV') of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested for the quarter ended December 31, 2025, compared to the 1.22% return of the Fund's benchmark, the Russell 2000® Growth Index 2 .
  • Security selection within the Health Care sector contributed to relative performance over the period, driven by an allocation to Guardant Health, Inc. (GH) (3.1%), a precision oncology company.
  • Security selection within the Industrials sector also contributed to relative performance, driven by an allocation to Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) (0.9%), a provider of launch services and space systems solutions.
  • Security selection within the Consumer Discretionary sector detracted from relative performance over the period, driven by an allocation to Stride, Inc. (LRN) (0.5%), an e-learning platform provider.
  • Security selection within the Consumer Staples sector also detracted from relative performance, driven by an allocation to Celsius Holdings, Inc. (CELH) (1.1%), a developer of energy drinks.

The portfolio weights shown in parenthesis are based on the average portfolio weight during the quarter for each security.

Performance data quoted reflect past performance and are no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance quoted. The investment return and principal value of an investment in the Fund will fluctuate so that shares, on any given day or when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. You can obtain performance data current to the most recent month end by calling Lord Abbett at (888) 522-2388 or referring to our website at Welcome | Lord, Abbett & Co LLC | Lord Abbett .

Positioning & Outlook

  • We believe that the secular bull market for innovative growth stocks continues. Taking a step back and evaluating the overall market: the pandemic inflation surge has passed, enabling the Fed to cut interest rates further this year – which is historically a positive for equity markets; earnings have proven to be resilient and continue to broaden out beyond the Magnificent 7; and secular forces, such as productivity gains from generative artificial intelligence (Gen AI), continue to provide markets an additional tailwind.
  • As we look ahead, innovation is flourishing in many areas of the US economy, and we are continuing to find quality companies across the market cap spectrum in these favored areas. These areas include but are not limited to pioneering Gen AI companies in semiconductors and software, as well as industrial companies enabling datacenter expansion and power infrastructure to support it. We are excited about AI's role in the emerging defense and space technology sector, as a historically low growth area sees an inflection in autonomous software and hardware systems. This positive inflection is also benefiting areas such as cloud, e-commerce, mobility, search, and social networks, as well as non-technology sectors such as Communication Services, Consumer, and Health Care.
  • That said, there are several countervailing factors still in play, such as policy uncertainty. Therefore, we believe security selection is more important than ever as high-quality businesses with strong business models and exceptional management teams should be better equipped to pass through tariffs, adjust supply chains, and find productivity gains from Gen AI tools.

data.title: Nationwide BNY Mellon Dynamic U.S. Equity Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Executive summary

  • The Fund returned 4.54% (IS share class) during Q4 vs. 3.81% for the Russell 1000® Value Index.
  • Over time the Fund seeks outperformance through access to two primary sources of alpha: equity income security selection and an index-based overlay. Stock selection within the equity income security sleeve contributed most to relative performance during the quarter.
  • For the equity income security selection, the largest overweights are currently in financials, healthcare, and energy.

Market Environment

In the US, equities generally advanced during the fourth quarter. The Dow Jones Industrial Average® increased by 4.03%, while the Nasdaq 100 Index® increased by 2.47%. US fixed-income markets also posted gains. Markets proved resilient amid elevated uncertainty driven by a prolonged federal government shutdown and ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. Inflation, as measured by the headline Consumer Price Index, eased gradually from 3.0% year-over-year in September to 2.7% year-over-year in November. Labor market data, while disrupted and delayed, pointed to slowing momentum rather than a sharp deterioration. Economic growth remained firm, with third-quarter gross domestic product ('GDP') rising at a solid 4.3% annualized, supported by consumer spending and government outlays despite softer retail sales and business investment. Against this backdrop, the Federal Reserve cut rates by 25 basis points (BPS) early in the quarter, while maintaining a cautious tone and reaffirming a data-dependent policy stance as markets looked ahead to 2026.

The S&P 500® Index increased by 2.66% for the quarter. Within the index, eight of the 11 Global Industry Classification Standard ('GICS') equity sectors finished in positive territory, led by health care and communication services. Value stocks outperformed growth stocks. Among other major equity benchmarks, the MSCI EAFE® Index, a measure of developed markets excluding the US and Canada, increased by 4.86%, while the MSCI Emerging Markets® Index increased by 4.73%.

Performance Review

Over time, the fund seeks outperformance through access to two primary sources of alpha: equity income security selection and an index-based asset allocation overlay. The fund outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Value, by +47 bps in the period posting an absolute return of +4.54%.

The equity income security selection component contributed an absolute return of +452 bps. Stock selection in the financials and materials sectors contributed positively over the period.

The asset allocation overlay was slightly positive during the quarter, adding +2 bps.

CONTRIBUTORS

Asset Allocation Overlay: US Equities +2.70% +351.0 bps
Freeport-McMoRan, Inc. (FCX) +29.60% +30.0 bps
Alcoa Corporation (AA) +49.70% +29.0 bps
Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) +13.20% +26.0 bps
Asset Allocation Overlay: US long Treasury Bonds +12.70% +2.0 bps

Source: Newton Investment Management North America, LLC, As of 12/31/25.

The results shown represent past performance; past performance does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the past performance shown.

DETRACTORS

Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) (included in US equity income security selection) - -32.0 bps
International Paper Company (IP) (included in US equity income security selection) -13.90% -27.0 bps

Source: Newton Investment Management North America, LLC, As of 12/31/25.

The results shown represent past performance; past performance does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the past performance shown.

Portfolio Positioning

For the equity income security selection, the largest overweights are currently in financials, health care, and energy. Given the differing subsectors, we believe the financials sector offers a wide range of investment opportunities. Our largest weighting within financials is currently US banks. We believe deregulation and the potential relaxation of Basel capital requirements will benefit the sector and boost capital markets activity. The capital return opportunity within the group remains highly compelling despite a more volatile economic outlook. We also have exposure to the capital markets companies, which we believe will benefit from both an increase in capital markets activity and the relaxation of capital requirements.

We are overweight health care with exposure across pharmaceuticals, managed care and medical devices. Within pharmaceuticals, we believe the recent deal signed with the US government on drug prices lifts a major overhang for the sector and will not have a material impact on earnings. Within managed care, we believe medical loss ratios have peaked and that the company's price increases will help stabilize margins. Within medical devices, we are focused on idiosyncratic opportunities in companies that have strong, and growing, market share and/or new product launches that will help drive future earnings.

We are overweight energy. Our holdings are diversified across integrated oils, natural gas, and refiners as well as exploration and production companies with attractive assets. We emphasize opportunities in high-quality exploration and production companies with solid balance sheets, low-cost and high-quality assets and compelling capital return targets.

The Fund maintained its defensive equity target allocation of 90%. Our estimate of the stock/bond premium continued to indicate that US equities are expensive, and ranged between 1.1% and 1.4%, well below its long-term average of 2.5%. The overlay continued to utilize equity call options for convexity and held an average implemented equity exposure of 100.2% over the quarter.

The asset allocation overlay also held an average allocation of 7.8% to long Treasuries over the quarter. The bond exposure is driven by carry, the term premium, and our tactical bond macro signal, with improved inflation the signal's main driver.

Buys and Sells

Security Name Rationale for Purchase
Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) The company operates as a semiconductor design and manufacturing company, developing analog ICs and embedded processors. We believe the company will benefit from a recovery in demand from their primary end-markets, including autos and industrials.
Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) Bristol-Myers Squibb is a global biopharmaceutical company that develops and delivers innovative medicines for serious diseases, with a focus on oncology, cardiovascular, immunology, and hematology therapies. We initiated a position as we believe they are attractively valued and well positioned to benefit from easing tariffs and drug pricing overhangs.
Dover Corporation (DOV) Dover Corporation manufactures industrial products and manufacturing equipment. The company's products include printing, identification, marking and coding systems, waste handling, industrial equipment, refrigeration systems, display cases, industrial pumps, fuel dispensers, nozzles, piping, and electronic tank gauge equipment. Dover serves customers worldwide and we believe the market is underappreciating their current order strength, which should help the company deliver earnings above current consensus expectations. The company has also restructured its portfolio of business to focus more on less-cyclical, higher margin and higher growth businesses.
Elevance Health, Inc. (ELV) Elevance Health operates as a health insurance provider. The company provides health, dental, vision, and pharmacy benefits, as well as life insurance, and disability insurance benefits. Elevance Health offers network-based managed care plans to large and small employers, individuals, Medicaid, and Medicare markets. We believe medical losses have started to plateau which should allow for recent price adjustments to stabilize earnings.
SLB Limited (SLB) SLB Ltd operates as an oilfield services company, providing scaling of new energy systems, delivering digital at scale, innovating in oil and gas, and decarbonizing industrial products and services, as well as offers harnessing geothermal energy, enterprise data performance, and methane elimination solutions. SLB serves customers worldwide and has large exposure to the offshore oil services market, where we expect demand to inflect more positive in the coming months. While not part of the original investment thesis, the company could be a large beneficiary of rebuilding Venezuela's oil business.
Security Name Rationale for Sale
Chevron Corporation (CVX) We eliminated the position in favor of more compelling risk/reward opportunities.
Labcorp Holdings Inc. (LH) We eliminated the position in favor of more compelling risk/reward opportunities.
Royal Caribbean Group (RCL) We eliminated the position in favor of more compelling risk/reward opportunities.
Intercontinental Exchange, Inc. (ICE) We eliminated the position in favor of more compelling risk/reward opportunities.
Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) We eliminated the position in favor of more compelling risk/reward opportunities.

OUTLOOK

Equity Income Security Selection Outlook

As we start the new year, we have an optimistic view on equity markets and value investing. This optimism is largely driven by the combination of cyclical, structural and secular factors that we believe are starting to align. Among these positive tailwinds, we believe fiscal policy, deregulation, and the broadening out of artificial intelligence ('AI') adoption will be the most notable and will likely benefit value-oriented sectors such as financials, healthcare, energy, and materials.

Within this positive stance, we continue to be mindful of some of the lingering risks that exist. These include “AI exuberance” and lofty expectations around those companies and sectors that are most levered to this exciting innovation. We especially want to be aware of and account for index and portfolio concentration in this area of the market. In addition, we will continue to monitor monetary policy and the market’s expectations for interest rates, which has been a source of volatility for the past few years. Geopolitical risk, which has already perked up to start 2026, is something that will also likely have an impact on markets and the names we own in the portfolios.

We believe our investment process is built for these types of environments as it helps us sort through the opportunities and risks to identify those companies that have the characteristics we believe can help drive returns as well as offer balance to a portfolio—something we have been recommending to investors for the past few years.

We continue to believe experienced active managers with long-tenured teams that employ a consistent and repeatable process are best suited for this type of environment, given a widening dispersion of potential macro outcomes and investment returns. It is also our view that investing in companies that sit at the nexus of strong and improving fundamentals, attractive valuation and catalyst-driven business momentum have the ability to deliver the best investment outcomes for our clients.

Asset Allocation Overlay Outlook

Our forecast for S&P 500 earnings-per-share growth over the next 12 months was 7.8% versus consensus expectations of 13.6%.

The bond exposure is driven by carry, the term premium, and our tactical bond macro signal, with improved inflation being the signal’s main driver.

References

  1. Average annual total returns are the annual compounded returns that would have produced the cumulative total return if fund performance had been constant during the given period.

data.title: UroGen: ZUSDURI Poised To Replace TURBT Surgery As SOC In Certain Bladder Cancers
data.description:
This is my seventh UroGen Pharma (URGN) article, following 11/2025's "UroGen: Expect Rough Patch Before Strong Recovery In 2026".

Knee-Deep in Its Rough Patch, Urogen's Stock is Moving Erratically

Price chart

As shown by its chart below, UroGen's share price, share volume, and market cap have gyrated up and down in recent months with little net overall change:

Shakespeare buffs might be mindful of Macbeth's famous lament as it applies to UroGen's share movements:

...a tale / Told by an idiot, full of sound and fury / signifying nothing...

Double Danger Zone—JELMYTO + ZUSDURI

The problem is Urogen is caught in a particularly treacherous version of the danger zone; the danger zone is that stretch between the time when a molecule is FDA approved until it proves its commercial viability. UroGen is actually suffering from a double dose of the danger zone.

Its JELMYTO (mitomycin gel) in the treatment of low-grade upper tract urothelial cancer (UTUC) was FDA approved on 04/15/2020. As discussed in Rough Patch, its revenues have never lived up to initial expectations. As I read the situation, they are unlikely ever to do so; it seems inevitable that JELMYTO is in an unending danger zone

UroGen's second approved molecule, its ZUSDURI, was FDA approved in 06/2025 for the treatment of low-grade intermediate-risk non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (LG-IR-NMIBC). Its initial Q3/2025 net product revenues were a nominal $1.8 million; Q4/2025 revenues were marginally improved at $14 million for Q4/2025.

ZUSDURI Permanent J Code

UroGen bulls such as myself are looking for significant improvement in ZUSDURI net product revenues in upcoming quarters. This is the anticipated scenario underlying the bullish thesis in Rough Patch. Effective 01/2026, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services' [CMS] permanent ZUSDURI J-Code will take effect. This J-Code will significantly simplify billing for ZUSDURI. As noted by CEO Barrett during its Q4/2025 conference call reported in 03/2026:

...we are encouraged by the early 2026 trajectory following the permanent J-code becoming effective January 1.

ZUSDURI revenue in 2025 was $15.8 million reflecting early launch dynamics as we work through typical reimbursement and operational steps necessary to build the foundation for broader adoption. With a permanent J-code now in place, a key barrier to adoption has been removed, facilitating more predictable patient access.

Since that milestone, as expected, we have seen a clear acceleration across key launch indicators, including the number of new and repeat prescribers, patient enrollment forms and new patient starts. Overall, we are pleased with how the launch is unfolding in early 2026, and we are on track with the assumptions we've made around its trajectory.

ZUSDURI Guidance

Possibly chastened by its previous overly optimistic estimates of anticipated JELMYTO revenues, UroGen has ventured no 2026 guidance for ZUSDURI revenues. In response to a question by a frustrated Piper Sandler analyst as to when such guidance might be given, CFO Degnan advised:

It is early in the launch, as we said, and there are certain variables that can affect the near-term uptake. So once we get a better visibility to steady-state demand, I would say, at least 2 quarters post the permanent J-code then we could consider providing formal guidance for the ZUSDURI.

In other words, possibly by Q2/2026, more likely by Q3/2026, investors should have useful insights on ZUSDURI's near-term performance. Impatient biotech investors are condemned to awful, persistent frustration if they can't learn to wait.

In ZUSDURI's instance, UroGen has advised that it is looking for it to reach peak sales of $1 billion. In response to a conference Call question wanting to connect peak sale potential to a time frame, Barrett deferred to Degnan, who responded:

...So I mean path to peak or time to peak, I would assume roughly 4 years to peak. So now that we have the J-code, a 4-year ramp to peak is reasonable.

Pharmakon Loan Revision Eases Liquidity—Reflects Strong Vote of Confidence in UroGen

Immediately prior to reporting its Q4/2025 earnings on 03/02/2026, UroGen issued a press release that it was revising its 03/2024 term loan with Pharmakon. The revised loan carries an interest rate of 8.25%, providing a significant savings compared to its initial loan's rate at 3-month LIBOR (with a 1.25% floor) plus 8.25%.

The new loan provides:

  1. $200 million funded at closing to refinance the existing $125 million loan with the balance of $75 million available for UroGen's use
  2. plus a $50 million tranche available at the Company’s discretion until 06/30/2027;
  3. in sum, providing $125 million of new liquidity for UroGen.

During its conference call, CFO Degnan reported that its cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities totaled $120.5 million at 12/31/2025. This 2025 holdover liquidity of $120.5 million, when added to the $125 million of net new cash available under the revised Pharmakon facility, takes its liquidity to $245.5 million.

This is an improvement but still seems a bit thin for a company that reported a cash burn of ~$162 million for 2025. At this rate its liquidity would dwindle down to $83.5 million by 12/31/2026.

While we lack ZUSDURI guidance for 2026, I submit that Pharmakon executives must have strong faith in its potential. Otherwise, why would they double Pharmakon's exposure while reducing the interest rate with no financial covenants? In this respect it is significant that Pharmakon is no parvenu to biotech lending. Rather, it has wide experience and exposure to financing deals with familiar biotech companies.

Pros and Cons to "Buy" Thesis in UroGen

Supporting Buy Thesis

The feature that attracts me most strongly to UroGen is the intuitively appealing significance of ZUSDORI's approach to treating LG-IR-NMIBC. This is a disease that:

...is the second most common urologic cancer in men. LG-IR-NMIBC represents approximately 22,000 newly diagnosed bladder cancer patients each year and an estimated 60,000 recurrences annually among patients diagnosed from previous years. Bladder cancer primarily affects older populations with increased risk of comorbidities, with the median age of diagnosis being 73 years. Guideline recommendations for the management of NMIBC include trans-urethral resection of bladder tumor [TURBT] as the standard of care. Up to 70 percent of NMIBC patients experience at least one recurrence and LG-IR-NMIBC patients are even more likely to recur and face repeated TURBT procedures. LG-IR-NMIBC is characterized by early or frequent recurrences, multiple tumors, or a solitary tumor larger than three centimeters.

As I evaluate this situation, speaking as one in the prime demographic for LG-IR-NMIBC, it seems inevitable that ZUSDURI supplant TURBT as SOC for IR-NMIBC. Who would not elect routine office procedures of 6 courses of ZUSDURI to a string of surgical procedures of unknown duration.

This is the calculus that underlies the $1 billion peak revenues by 2030, which bulls are relying on to support their UroGen investments. As I write on 03/03/2026, UroGen has a market cap of ~0.92 billion. This is way too low for a company with an approved drug credibly expected to generate $1 billion in revenues by the end of the decade.

Risks to a Buy Thesis

Sadly, there is always a counterweight to one's firmest-held market positions. Sometimes there are issues that could be anticipated; sometimes they come out of nowhere. I will not touch on the latter as regards UroGen. Rather, I will address those that are foreseeable.

First and foremost, let me confess that I have misjudged the market potential of more than one medical device or therapy that has been unable to gather market traction. In some cases these are caused by management miscues resulting in failed launches or financial failure. UroGen's record to date leaves it exposed on both fronts. Its JELMYTO product is dead in the water, as I assess the situation. Its liquidity is OK, but it will only stay OK if ZUSDURI succeeds.

A second issue of potential concern is ZUSDURI's intellectual property. According to its latest 10-K, ZUSDURI's regulatory exclusivity expires in 06/2028. This means that its patent protection is of heightened interest. In this regard, the 10K advises:

...we own three issued U.S. patents and two issued patents in Europe. These issued patents are expected to expire in 2031. Moreover, we filed two new U.S. patent applications with the USPTO, that relate to compositions comprising Zusduri and for the method of treating bladder cancer.

Conclusion

I have tried to communicate UroGen's exciting potential based on its FDA-approved ZUSDURI. As a UroGen shareholder, I recognize that it is not an "own and forget" type of investment. Rather, it bears careful monitoring to watch ZUSDURI's sales.

This is important from the standpoint of monitoring its liquidity and also to assess how the urology practitioner community and the LG-IR-NMIBC patient community are reacting to ZUSDURI.

data.title: GS GQG Partners International Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

During the 4Q25 period, the MSCI ACWI increased 3.29% with nine of the 11 sectors exhibiting a positive return. The largest drivers of the benchmark's gain were Healthcare (+9.9%, contributing 86 bps to the performance of the ACWI index), Financials (+5.0%, +85 bps), and Information Technology (+2.9%, +77 bps). From a country perspective, there were increases in 18 of the 20 largest ACWI index constituents led by the US (MSCI US +2.4%, contributing 162 bps to the performance of the benchmark) and South Korea (+27.4%, +31 bps).

Stylistically, the MSCI ACWI Value Index (+3.66%) outperformed MSCI ACWI Growth (+2.84%). Regarding the other primary equity benchmarks, the MSCI ACWI Ex USA, MSCI Emerging Markets, and S& am p;P 500 indices increased 5.05%, 4.73%, and 2.66%, respectively.

In the US, we believe a strong 3Q25 earnings season and an ac com modative central bank were the primary drivers of the rise in the equity market. According to FactSet, S&P 500 companies generated year-over-year earnings growth of 13.6% for the quarter ending in September and reported during the 4Q25 period.

The Federal Reserve lowered the overnight policy rate by 25 bps in each of two separate FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee) meetings for a total easing of 50 bps during the December quarter. The target Fed funds rate entering 2026 was 3.50-3.75%. The Fed chairman noted in December that the US labor market "seems to have significant downside risks" as the unemployment rate increased to 4.6% in November 2025, its highest level since September 2021.

The central bank also expressed its belief that the Bureau of Labor Statistics could be overstating the growth in nonfarm payrolls by up to 60,000 jobs a month. Coincidentally, nonfarm payroll employment increased by 64,000 jobs in November.

In South Korea, we believe the equity market had a strong quarter due to investor optimism on AI-related investments as the rally was led by two names in the tech sector that share an oligopoly with a US competitor in the design of high bandwidth memory chips.

In October, these South Korean companies signed letters of intent to participate in the US Stargate infrastructure project and co-develop AI data centers with OpenAI in South Korea. The US competitor subsequently reported strong earnings for the quarter ending in November and significantly increased guidance for the current quarter due to strong demand and increases in end market pricing. As a result, shares of each of the three companies increased meaningfully.

The US 10-year Treasury yield entered the quarter at 4.15%, troughed in mid-October at 3.95%, and ended the period at 4.17%. Investment grade credit spreads in the US widened by three bps in 4Q25 to 79 bps, while high yield spreads increased one bp to 281 bps. The Bloomberg US Aggregate bond index (largely US Treasuries, investment grade corporates, and agency mortgage-backed securities) declined 0.4% during the quarter. The US Dollar Index ('DXY') strengthened 0.5% in the period.

The Bloomberg Commodity Index increased 4.8% during 4Q25 as gains in copper (+17.4%) and gold (+12.1%) were only partially offset by losses in crude oil (Brent -7.9%). Copper prices rallied as supply has been impacted by unexpected disruptions at significant mines in Chile, Peru, and Indonesia in 2025 while demand appears to be firm led by data center construction and investments in power grid infrastructure. Gold acted as a safe-haven asset relative to the US dollar due to the country's record debt levels and the perception of waning central bank independence. We believe the oil price was weak due to concerns of excess supply and slowing economic momentum in China as industrial production came in weaker than expected.

Drivers of Return

During 4Q25, the GS GQG Partners International Opportunities Fund underperformed the MSCI ACWI ex-USA (NET) benchmark by 111 basis points net of fees. The portfolio had a net return of 3.94% versus the benchmark's 5.05%.

From a sector perspective, during the period the fund was helped on a relative basis by an underweight to Consumer Discretionary, stock selection in Health Care, and stock selection in Communication Services. Relative performance was negatively impacted by an overweight to Information Technology, stock selection in Financials, and stock selection in Energy.

From a regional perspective, the fund was helped on a relative basis by an overweight to Asia (ex-Japan) and stock selection in Europe (ex-UK). Relative performance was negatively impacted by stock selection in Emerging Markets and Canada.

It operates a market for customer shopping, a third-party marketing platform for brands as well as retailers, and a cloud computing business. Investors appeared to rotate from certain AI-related names in November to more defensive areas including healthcare and consumer staples. The stock continued to slide in late November after a negative earnings surprise of over 10% in 3Q25. This is a relative contributor as we did not own this name during the quarter.

We believe investor sentiment toward the shares has also improved due to optimism about the company's ability to raise power prices during the next few years.

We believe the shares fell due to concern from a sell-side analyst that a likely upward revision to Tencent's AI-related capital expenditure may result in higher depreciation costs and modest pressure on the company's operating margins. This was a relative contributor as we were overweight the name during the period.

Chubb is headquartered in Switzerland. During 4Q25, an insurance industry publication reported that Chubb made an informal takeover approach for AIG (American International Group, Inc (AIG)). Key Relative Detractors:

Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF) - Samsung Electronics is a diversified electronics conglomerate that manufactures a variety of products including semiconductors, smartphones, home appliances, medical equipment, and telecom hardware. The company is based in South Korea. Samsung's stock increased for two reasons. First, the company signed a letter of intent to participate in the US Stargate infrastructure project and co-develop AI data centers with OpenAI in South Korea. Second, Samsung shares an oligopoly with SK Hynix and Micron Technology in the design of high bandwidth memory chips. Micron reported strong earnings for the quarter ending in November and significantly increased guidance for the current quarter due to strong demand and increases in end market pricing. As a result, the stocks of each of the three companies increased meaningfully. Samsung was a relative detractor as we did not own this name during the quarter.

SK Hynix Inc. (HXSCL) - SK Hynix manufactures, distributes, and sells memory and system semiconductors. The company is based in South Korea. SK Hynix shares an oligopoly with Samsung and Micron Technology in the design of high bandwidth memory chips. Micron reported strong earnings for the quarter ending in November and significantly increased guidance for the current quarter due to strong demand and increases in end market pricing. As a result, the stocks of each of the three companies increased meaningfully. This is a relative detractor as we did not own this name during the quarter.

Philip Morris International Inc. - Philip Morris International manufactures cigarettes, smoke-free products, associated electronic devices, and other nicotine-containing products primarily in markets outside the United States. For the 3Q25 period, revenue and EPS exceeded expectations by 2% and 7%, respectively. However, for the company's Zyn-branded nicotine pouches, PM is increasing promotional activity to maintain its leading market share while de-stocking inventory at retailers to normalize supply after adding manufacturing capacity for that business during the last year. As a result, management trimmed its guidance for 4Q25 EPS growth from 16% to 8%.

Enbridge Inc. - Enbridge is an energy infrastructure company based in Canada. It operates four business segments across North America including liquids pipelines, natural gas transmission, natural gas distribution, and renewable power generation. Enbridge reported 3Q25 EBITDA and EPS that missed the consensus forecasts by 1% and 12%, respectively, due to lower than expected contributions from its gas distribution as well as its renewables segments. However, the company re-affirmed its prior calendar 2025 guidance for EBITDA.

The firm is headquartered in Brazil. We were overweight Petrobras's stock during 4Q25, a period when the shares underperformed the benchmark. The shares fell in-line with the decline in crude oil prices in December. We believe the commodity price was weak due to concerns of excess supply and slowing economic momentum in China as industrial production in November came in weaker than expected.

Positioning and Potential Headwinds

The Consumer Staples and Utilities sectors are the largest overweights in the portfolio with the Information Technology and Industrials sectors the largest underweights. Potential headwinds include the recent increase in tariffs by the Trump administration. If there is a tariff induced slowdown in economic activity, that may result in lower consumer spending.

In addition, utility companies face regulatory risks including rate determinations and approvals of cap ex plans as they seek to add power generation, transmission, and distribution capacity. Another potential headwind is a continued increase in AI-related spending that could drive positive EPS revisions in the tech sector, which would likely impact our relative performance. We expect to monitor the relevant data closely and adapt to the changing environment.

On a country basis, the United States, the United Kingdom, and India are the largest overweights while Japan and China are the largest underweights. Potential headwinds include a deteriorating fiscal situation in the UK with high debt levels and a growing budget deficit that may weigh on economic growth. Another would be a weather shock in India that could adversely impact agricultural output, raise food prices, and curtail consumer spending in other areas.

Outlook

We are overweight the Consumer Staples and Utilities sectors. In Consumer Staples, we believe the fundamentals for certain tobacco businesses are solid with growth at some companies coming from next generation products. In addition, select tobacco stocks have what we consider to be attractive dividend yields. Regarding Utilities, we believe the European regulatory environment for power utilities appears to be improving as companies seek an acceptable rate of return on the investment required to grow the supply of electricity. Meanwhile, we have identified several utilities focused on power transmission where we believe there is high visibility on revenue and earnings.

We are also overweight Energy as we believe certain exploration and development companies are taking a disciplined approach to production growth while consistently returning capital to shareholders in the form of dividends and stock buybacks. In addition, we like select midstream companies, as we view these pipeline operators as utility-like businesses that may generate high single-digit earnings growth while their stocks offer what we consider to be attractive dividend yields.

We are underweight Information Technology. We are skeptical that the current run rate of AI-related cap ex spending is sustainable and believe certain tech names are overearning. In addition, many stocks in the sector are expensive, in our opinion, with a risk of compression in their valuation multiples.

Potential headwinds include the recent increase in tariffs by the Trump administration. If there is a tariff induced slowdown in economic activity, that may result in lower consumer spending. Utility companies face regulatory risks including rate determinations and approvals of cap ex plans as they seek to add power generation, transmission, and distribution capacity. A continued increase in AI-related spending could drive positive EPS revisions in the tech sector, which would likely impact our relative performance. We expect to monitor the relevant data closely and adapt to the changing environment.

While we are not economists, we do monitor macro data as part of our risk framework. We underwrite the existing, and potential, companies in our portfolios focusing on the visibility of their future free cash flows. Ignoring the macro conditions in which these companies operate would impact the accuracy of any investor's earnings outlook, in our opinion. However, adapting our portfolios to reflect new information is a hallmark of our process and we will continue to go where the data leads us.

data.title: AMG GW&K ESG Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Drivers

The AMG GW&K ESG Bond Fund (Class N) returned 0.89% in the fourth quarter of 2025, compared with the 1.10% return of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. For the 12 months ending December 31, 2025, the Fund returned 7.26%, compared with the 7.30% return for the benchmark. Please note that this Fund has multiple share classes.

Market Overview

The quarter began with a federal government shutdown, the longest in U.S. history, creating a lack of official data releases and reducing visibility around the Federal Reserve Board's (the Fed's) willingness to lower rates. Despite this uncertainty, the market looked to other available data sources to reinforce a view that a weakening jobs market overshadowed stubbornly high, but moderating inflation. Ultimately, the Fed delivered on their second and third consecutive cuts, stressing their priority to manage near-term labor market risks while striking a less hawkish policy path than feared. The third quarter earnings season broadly exceeded expectations, delivering the fourth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth, painting the picture of an economy that remains resilient. A backdrop of an accommodative Fed, strong corporate fundamentals, and the lowest rate volatility in four years, allowed front-end treasury rates to edge lower while boosting most risk markets.

The Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index returned 1.1% for the quarter, capping off the best annual return since 2020 at 7.3%. The securitized sector led the way with agency fixed rate mortgage-backed securities ('MBS') spreads outperforming, benefiting from the reduced volatility in rates markets. Investment grade corporates managed to produce positive excess returns by offsetting modest spread widening with their additional carry over treasuries. The treasury curve continued to steepen, led by lower front-end rates with the two- and five-year declining 13 and two basis points, respectively, while 10- and 30-year rates were up two and 11 basis points. The 2s30s yield curve ended the year at its steepest level as the front end continued to be anchored by accommodative Fed policy while the long end faced investor fears over inflation, fiscal supply concerns, and U.S. Dollar demand issues.

During the fourth quarter, concerns that idiosyncratic credit losses at lower-quality companies could broaden into systemic issues across loans and private credit contributed to a modest early sell-off. Growing questions about returns on artificial intelligence ('AI') infrastructure investments added pressure before risk markets rebounded on renewed rate cut optimism and moderating inflation. Investment grade spreads ended the quarter at 78 open-adjusted spreads ('OAS'), only a few basis points away from multi-decade lows, returning 0.8%. High yield spreads at 266 OAS were essentially unchanged, with cash conversion cycles (CCCs) underperforming in a reversal of last quarter's strong lower-rated performance. Double-B and single-B spreads were slightly tighter, however, driving the High Yield Index to return 1.3%, slightly outperforming the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index. Corporate credit spreads continue to reflect limited downside risk amid low recession odds and a benign default outlook. Further, macro conditions remain supportive of investor demand and credit fundamentals, with risks more likely credit-specific than macro-related. However, a significant rise in net issuance next year introduces a new technical uncertainty.

Securitized products delivered strong performance in the fourth quarter, led by the outperformance of the agency MBS sector. MBS spreads tightened to multi-year lows as persistently high mortgage rates constrained supply, while a steeper yield curve and declining rate volatility boosted demand. Overall, the outlook for securitized markets remains constructive, as the conclusion of quantitative tightening and the Fed's initiation of reserve management operations in December are supportive of more rangebound rate volatility and sustained demand heading into the new year.

Fund Performance Drivers

The Fund underperformed the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index for the quarter. Underperformance was driven by an out-of-benchmark allocation to high yield and an overweight to corporates. This was partially offset by security selection within corporates, particularly in the BBB-rated technology sector. The Fund's overweight to preferreds were also modest positive contributors. The effects from duration and yield curve positioning were minimal.

Outlook and Positioning

As we enter 2026, we are mindful of this administration's meaningful efforts to further bolster various weaknesses in the economy both through their actions in the capital markets and fiscal policy. At recent times of crisis, the markets have abided by the rule of "don't fight the Fed" and looked for supportive monetary policy actions. Now, we've added other areas of the government to that support, and it has been evidenced with falling volatility in the capital markets. This backdrop looks to continue to be supportive of credit and securitized markets in the coming year, especially as the growth in technological advancements creates productivity gains and alters financial landscapes. Despite fears of global conflicts, an explosion of AI-driven debt supply, and markets that have priced much of the good news in, stable and reasonable fixed-income yields look to make this another year where thoughtful active management can find attractive value in corporates and high-grade securitized products.

data.title: LKQ Corp.: More Things To Be Fixed Before This Can Be A Buy
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on LKQ Corporation (LKQ) was a hold rating because I saw no strong catalyst that would result in multiples getting re-rated upwards. The setup is better than before, in my view. Specialty has clearly improved, and North America continues to take share in a weak market. However, I do not think LKQ has done enough to justify an upgrade since organic growth remains weak, margins are still under pressure, and Europe is not fixed.

4Q25 Results Update

LKQ's Q4 headline revenue number looked better than it really was. Total revenue grew 2.7% y/y on a reported basis, with total parts and services revenue up 2.2%, but that number included a 3.7% FX tailwind. Organically, parts and services revenue was down 1.7%. Splitting by regions, North America remains as the best-performing part (relatively) of the business. Parts and services revenue was down 0.7% organically here, while Europe's organic growth was down 4.8%. By segments, Specialty did really well with organic growth of 7.8%. Other than this, there weren't really any other positives. EBIT fell 40.9% y/y, while segment EBITDA was down to $321 million from $392 million. Net income from continuing operations was also down 50% y/y, and diluted EPS went from $0.58 to $0.29, while adj. diluted EPS fell to $0.59 from $0.78.

Areas That Have Turned Better

Looking ahead, there are reasons to turn optimistic. One of the negative points I made before is that there was no obvious near-term catalyst that could shift sentiment. That is no longer true. While management was not explicit that they would do it, they mentioned the possibility of a potential sale of the company (which is still in process to explore a sale of the Specialty segment). While the timing of this happening is virtually impossible to know, it does help with the stock sentiment (forming a valuation floor). On the part about the sale of Specialty, the value of it should have increased since Q4 saw the second consecutive quarter of organic growth. LKQ is no longer trying to sell a segment that looks like it is collapsing. The operating trend is better now, which at least improves the valuation during any sale process.

North America also looks better from here. We now have more evidence that LKQ's share gains are real, and I would expect more share gains from here. The key line that led me to this view is that management said LKQ gained share by deepening relationships with MSOs (multi-shop operators) and insurers, and they also said repairable claims improved from ~-10% in 1Q to -4% to -6% in Q4. This tells us that the market was still obviously weak, but things have improved over the months, and that sets up easier comps for FY2026 (especially while LKQ is still winning wallet share). On top of that, management said volumes sold to MSO customers were up in the teens in FY2025, while MSO volumes overall (industry level) were only flat to slightly higher (i.e., LKQ gained a ton of share here).

Reason to Stay Neutral

The main reason is that this business is still not showing signs of growth. Organic revenue growth is down across both regions, and total segment EBITDA margin contracted by 240 bps to 9.7%. We can point out all the positive developments underneath the hood, but if the P&L is not showing, I don't think the market will attach any value to them. This is especially the case for Europe, where management said that demand weakness was due to soft consumer confidence, macro uncertainty, and pricing pressure. The first two are not new since Europe has been in this uncertain territory for the recent history, but pricing pressure is something new to me. It suggests that demand must have deteriorated significantly, so much that LKQ has to cut prices to capture volume.

The fortunate news for the bulls is that LKQ has acted accordingly. They expanded private-label inventory with introductory pricing (which helps with lowering effective prices while protecting margins), reviewed >85% of the Europe SKU portfolio, delisted 71,000 SKUs, and said a key system integration is planned for early 2Q26. If all goes well, we should see Europe starting to print positive comps. LKQ also kickstarted a restructuring plan that is expected to generate more than $50 million of annualized cost savings. String all these together, and I would say the setup is better than my last write-up. But the problem has not gone away. Management also said these pricing and private-label actions are pressuring revenue and margins in the near term. So, Europe has a visible path to recovery today, but it is not yet easier to underwrite.

Valuation

As discussed, there are reasons to lean bullish after this Q4 performance. We finally have a proper catalyst in sight; Specialty is proving to be rebounding nicely, LKQ continues to win share in North America, and there is a plan to turn things around in Europe. It makes sense that the market reacted positively, pushing valuation up from 9.5x NTM PE to the ~10.5x today. I won't challenge that view, but from here, I think the path from 10.5x to 13x (the historical average) will need more proper execution. LKQ must show tangible improvements in organic growth and margin (both of which are still poor today). It is also hard to expect multiples to go any higher when one of LKQ's peers, Genuine Parts Company (GPC), trades at 15x with mid-single-digit revenue growth expectations (LKQ is at low-single-digits).

Conclusion

My view for LKQ remains a hold rating. I think this quarter was better than the last in a few ways. LKQ now has a catalyst, Specialty is no longer a drag, and North America share gains are real and should last. But the main issue is that the improvement still is not showing up clearly enough in the P&L. Organic growth is still negative, margins are still weak, and Europe remains the biggest overhang.

data.title: PGIM Quant Solutions Large-Cap Core Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. equities posted modest gains for the quarter, closing 2025 firmly in positive territory.

Inflation remained above target, but the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) lowered its target rate twice during the quarter, prompted by a softening labor market.

Unprofitable artificial intelligence ('AI')-related companies initially attracted investor enthusiasm early in the quarter before experiencing a sharp revaluation in November, signaling a shift away from speculative growth towards fundamentals.

Despite late-December weakness in major indexes, the Russell 2000 returned just over 2% for the quarter, and the S&P 500 and S&P 400 indexes returned 2.66% and 1.64%, respectively.

Strong factor performance in the large-cap space boosted relative returns. However, value and growth factors weighed on relative performance in small-cap portfolios. Factor performance was mixed in the mid-cap space.

Quarter

After a turbulent start driven by global trade tensions, investors remained in risk-on mode for much of the year, fueled by easing monetary policy, robust corporate earnings, and AI hype. Following a strong January, U.S. equities turned markedly negative amid escalating trade war tensions, culminating in a broad market sell-off in early April. Markets then rallied meaningfully as trade tensions eased, setting the stage for U.S. equities to climb to new record highs for the remainder of the year, despite the persistent realities of stretched valuations and above-target inflation.

Monetary policy remained a key driver of market volatility as investors anticipated the Fed's next move. Price pressures lingered throughout the year, complicating policymakers' efforts to support economic growth while simultaneously tempering inflation. The consumer price index ('CPI') began the year near 3%, fluctuated throughout the year, and stood at 2.7% in November, still above the Fed's long-run 2% target. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate increased by 0.5% year-over-year to 4.6% in November, its highest level since 2021. Coupled with a slowdown in job growth, the softening labor market was the primary catalyst for the Fed's three rate cuts in the second half of the year.

Market leadership remained narrow. Despite underperforming the broad market during the first quarter, mega-cap stocks, most notably Alphabet (GOOGL) and Nvidia (NVDA), rebounded sharply and ultimately outpaced the broader index. Strong earnings and the monetization of AI initiatives were key drivers of the outperformance.

By November, this small group of stocks reached historically elevated levels of concentration, accounting for approximately 36% of the S&P 500 Index. Valuation dispersion also remained wide, with the forward-looking Price-to-Earnings (P/E) multiple of the Russell 1000 Growth Index nearly double that of the Value Index. Despite this highly concentrated environment, our large-cap strategy fared well.

The period was unique for small-cap equities. Following a weak start to the year, small-cap stocks outperformed large-cap stocks during the third quarter, fueled by optimism surrounding easing monetary policy. In July, investors rotated into low-quality, speculative stocks, sparking a short-lived junk rally. The rally broadened in September and October as investors shifted into unprofitable tech companies, hoping to capture AI-related growth tailwinds. These stocks reversed during November as concerns grew around a potential bubble. Despite this correction, non-earners within the Russell 2000 Index outperformed their positive-earning counterparts by more than 10% for the full year, a phenomenon last observed in 2020.

Though the third quarter market rally was broad, mega-cap tech stocks remained the front-runners, driven by AI enthusiasm and strong earnings results. The Russell 1000 Growth Index returned over 10%, compared to around 5% for the Russell 1000 Value Index. The valuation disparity widened for the quarter, with the forward-looking P/E multiple of the Russell 1000 Growth Index rising above 35, almost double that of the Value Index.

Factor efficacy was challenged during the first quarter and into early April, as investors indiscriminately sold stocks regardless of fundamentals amid heightened market noise driven by ongoing tariff developments. Investors rotated out of expensive tech and into low volatility stocks, reflecting a risk-off mentality. These conditions were particularly difficult to navigate in our large- and mid-cap strategies. In the small-cap space, value and quality factors experienced notable periods of underperformance, particularly in September and October, when low-quality, expensive stocks led returns. This dynamic also weighed on mid-cap returns. Such market conditions present headwinds for an alpha-seeking, diversified strategy like ours. Factor performance improved into year end, particularly in the large-cap space, where value factors staged a notable rebound as investors rotated into undervalued areas of the market.

The year as a whole presented no shortage of challenges, marked by bouts of volatility, heightened concentration, and speculative rallies. Against this backdrop, our small- and mid-cap portfolios lagged their respective benchmarks, while our large-cap portfolios trailed modestly for the year.

Despite these short-term headwinds, relative performance has been very strong over the trailing five years, reflecting the durability of our investment process across varying market environments.

Looking ahead, uncertainty persists across several fronts, most notably from geopolitical tensions, the pace of Fed easing, and the sustainability of AI-driven revenue growth. Nevertheless, we firmly believe our portfolios are well-positioned to navigate these evolving market conditions.

Outlook and Positioning

PGIM Quantitative Solutions employs a quantitative, bottom-up investment process. Our proprietary stock selection model utilizes a carefully chosen suite of value, growth and quality factors with strong theoretical underpinnings to evaluate a broad universe of stocks. Our process is adaptive. Factors are dynamically weighted to reflect differences in individual stock growth rates. This ensures we are examining stocks through the right lenses.

Using the relative scores from the stock selection model, PGIM Quantitative Solutions constructs a Fund that efficiently targets factor exposures, while minimizing unintended risks and transactions costs. The result is a diversified core Fund that is positioned to produce consistent alpha over time.

data.title: USANA Looks Like An Undervalued Turnaround Stock
data.description:
USANA Health Sciences (USNA) is in the middle of a turnaround. USANA’s main business segment, its MLM, may be in permanent decline. In general, MLMs aren’t doing very well right now. So, like other MLMs, the company is pivoting. USANA has created two other businesses, its Hiya direct-to-customer brand and its Rise Wellness brand, that are rapidly growing. The company appears to be using its core MLM segment as a cash cow to fund its growth businesses, and its Q4 2025 results indicate that this strategy is working.

Because of Hiya and Rise Wellness, the company as a whole reported growing revenue in 2025. In Q4 2025, USANA reported that its revenue rose 5.9% to $226.2 million. The company also reported a GAAP loss of $1.8 million for the quarter, but it also reported $27.8 million in adjusted EBITDA. USANA is incurring additional expenses right now because its business model is changing, so I think it is appropriate to use adjusted EBITDA for this company. For example, USANA reported $7.0 million in impairment charges for the quarter, which is a non-cash expense, and it’s also larger than the GAAP loss the company reported. So, on a non-GAAP basis, this company is still profitable.

It’s likely that USANA will report a profit in 2026 as well. While SA says that Wall Street analysts aren’t covering this stock right now, it currently has a forward P/E of 10. So, right now this could be a growth stock that’s priced like a value stock. USANA’s new business segments are already generating growth, and the company has some big partners that will help it continue to generate growth in 2026.

Hiya is Collaborating With Disney to Make Kids’ Vitamins

USANA’s Hiya subsidiary appears to have a long-term collaboration deal with Disney (DIS). According to MediaPost, Hiya launched Lion King vitamins in the spring of 2025 and then launched Disney Princess vitamins during the summer. While these might sound like limited-time offers, I visited Hiya’s website, and the Disney-branded vitamins are still available there. So, while management didn’t say anything about upcoming collaborations on the Q4 2025 call, there may be more collaborations between Disney and Hiya in the future. The Hiya vitamins are clean-label products that are sweetened with sugar alternatives, so the brand is on-trend.

And unlike some of the other companies I’ve covered recently, USANA is unlikely to disappoint investors with a collaboration that doesn’t perform well. Kids need to take multivitamins on a regular schedule, so vitamins aren’t a one-time purchase. Additionally, Hiya is selling its vitamins through a subscription service. So, customers who order vitamins from this company are likely to order them again. As of the end of Q4 2025, Hiya has 181,700 subscribers.

USANA reported that Hiya generated $30 million in sales during Q4 2025 and expects Hiya to report full-year 2026 sales in the $140-$155 million range. So, Hiya could generate $120 million in annualized sales right now based on its fourth-quarter results and is expected to generate $147.5 million in annual sales at the midpoint in 2026. That’s a 23% revenue growth rate.

Rise Wellness is Also Gaining Wider Distribution for Protein Pop

USANA also expects Rise Wellness to contribute $65-$80 million to its revenue in 2026. This brand’s main product is Protein Pop, a carbonated drink with whey protein and collagen. It looks like Protein Pop could scale up very quickly because USANA has already convinced Costco (COST) and Target (TGT) to stock the drinks. So, this is another new product that could make a significant contribution to USANA’s revenue in 2026.

Meanwhile, the company’s inventory of protein drinks is rising because it’s preparing to distribute these drinks to the stores. This is another reason why the company’s recent GAAP results might be misleading. It’s carrying more inventory than it did in previous years, and that includes the Hiya vitamins that are now stored in its warehouses as well. USANA’s inventory rose from $69.7 million in Q4 2024 to $102.6 million in Q4 2025.

The Core USANA Business is Still Shrinking

In January 2026, USANA released preliminary guidance that implies that its core segment, its MLM, will report weaker sales in 2026. The company said that its core segment generated sales of around $777 million in 2025 but will generate sales of $720 to $765 million in 2026. So, at the midpoint, the company expects its core segment sales to fall 4% to $742.5 million.

My thesis for USANA is that the growth from Hiya and Rise Wellness will make up for the declining sales of USANA’s core MLM segment. This is similar to my long-term thesis for Simply Good Foods (SMPL), which owns the shrinking Atkins brand as well as the growing Quest and OWYN brands. While I still think Simply Good Foods has good long-term growth potential because of this setup, the company is experiencing margin compression right now, and that’s also a risk factor for USANA.

USANA’s Pivot is a Risk Factor

USANA’s core segment still operates as an MLM, but now the company is selling vitamins directly to its customers, and its protein drinks are available at major retail stores. In its earnings report, the company also described its MLM segment as a legacy channel. Specifically, the USANA CEO said, “We are leveraging our strong foundation in the nutrition business and expanding beyond our legacy channel to connect with a greater number of health-conscious consumers worldwide.”

USANA also used to refer to its MLM members as associates, and then it changed that to brand partners in Q2 2025. Now, it sounds like it’s calling them active customers. In the Q4 2025 report, the company said it has 387,000 active customers, down from 454,000 a year ago. So, it sounds like USANA plans to transition away from the MLM model. This could be a good long-term move for the company, but some of its MLM members might be unhappy about this. If these members leave earlier than expected, USANA might report weaker results in 2026.

Comparative Valuation for USANA

Because of the uncertainty involved with its pivot, this company is trading at very low valuations right now. After the market close on March 3, USANA had a forward EV/EBITDA of 2.67 and a forward P/FCF ratio of 7.31. It’s also trading at a P/B ratio of 0.69. While other MLMs are also trading at low valuations right now, USANA is still trading at a discount to peers. Herbalife’s forward EV/EBITDA is 5.58, and Nature’s Sunshine has a forward EV/EBITDA of 8.53.

My USANA Valuation

In the fourth quarter, USANA reported an adjusted EBITDA margin of 12.3%. It’s likely that because of its change in business model, as well as rising input costs, its margins will be lower in 2026. So, I’ll project that USANA’s EBITDA margin will drop 200 basis points to 10.3% in 2026. The midpoint of its guidance calls for $962.5 million in revenue, so I’d project that its EBITDA will be $99.1 million in 2026. This is actually lower than the company’s EBITDA guidance for 2026, but I’m being conservative here.

But this company is also trading at a massive discount to other MLMs and food companies, so I’d project that its EV/EBITDA will rise to 3.5 in 2026 as well. That gives me a projected EV of $347 million. USANA’s capital structure is also a bit more complex than other companies’ capital structures. At the end of Q4 2025, USANA had $158 million in cash. The company had no long-term debt, but it did have a $14 million line of credit liability. And it also reported $53 million in redeemable non-controlling interest because of the 2024 Hiya acquisition. Non-controlling shareholders still have a 21.2% stake in Hiya and will have the right to force USANA to buy their remaining stake in the future. So, if I add USANA’s cash back to its EV and subtract its line of credit balance and the non-controlling interest, I get a 2026 market cap projection of $438 million.

USANA has also been buying back stock, and its diluted share count has dropped from 25 million shares in 2016 to 18.6 million shares in 2025. So, I’ll project that USANA’s share count will drop to 18.0 million at the end of 2026 as well. After dividing a market cap of $438 million by 18.0 million shares, I get a one-year target price of $24.33 per share. USANA’s closing price on March 3 was $19.45 per share, so my target price is 25% higher than the current price.

Again, Wall Street analysts aren’t covering this stock right now, but SA analysts give it a hold rating.

Conclusion

USANA’s pivot appears to be working. While the company’s core MLM segment is slowly shrinking and will probably continue to shrink, its two growth segments have the potential to grow rapidly. Hiya, in particular, appears to be a very good brand. And Rise Wellness could also succeed because of rising demand for protein drinks and expanded distribution in 2026. Meanwhile, USANA is trading at a very low valuation and has a strong balance sheet. While this company’s future does appear uncertain, it seems like the reward could outweigh the risks here. So, I say that USANA is a buy.

data.title: UVXY And SVXY: Trading Volatility In A Turbulent Market
data.description:
The ProShares Ultra VIX Short-Term Futures ETF (UVXY) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with amplified exposure to the daily performance of the S&P 500 VIX Short-Term Futures Index. With the current market turmoil and geopolitical tensions building, I believe volatility will remain heightened for the time being, providing investors with an opportunity to exploit short-term dispersions using UVXY. Strategically, traders may balance the upside and downside risk with the ProShares Short VIX Short-Term Futures ETF (SVXY) to capture the decline in volatility. Given the speculative nature of volatility and the leveraged risk involved in these strategies, traders should solely utilize these ETFs for daily exposure; holding leveraged strategies for longer than a single trading day may expose investors to substantial and potentially unrecoverable losses.
